skill.name: make-automation
skill.description: Use Rube MCP to inspect Make operations, list supported languages, and list valid timezones for Make automation workflows.
skill.summary: Inspect Make operations and validate Make language or timezone values through Rube MCP.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but these are false positives from markdown backticks, Make tool names, and operational monitoring text. The confirmed risk is legitimate third-party network use: users are instructed to connect Rube MCP and authenticate a Make account, which can expose Make operations metadata to that service. No prompt injection, malicious code, credential theft, or local command execution evidence was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP Server and Make Account Access
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to add https://rube.app/mcp as an MCP server and authenticate the Make toolkit. This matches the skill purpose, but it creates a real data-sharing risk because Make operations metadata and account-scoped access may pass through a third-party MCP provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoint and Make authentication flow are explicit in the skill text. The use is legitimate rather than malicious, so the confidence supports medium risk instead of high risk.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Tool References
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are inline code spans and fenced workflow examples naming Rube and Make tools. No evidence found of local shell execution, Ruby code, subprocess invocation, or command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible markdown instructions and numbered examples. They do not contain executable script syntax beyond formatting backticks.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts occur on descriptive Make automation text, enum lookup text, operations metadata, and token-authentication discussion. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, custom encryption, or any cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows product and workflow terminology, not cryptographic code. The skill is a markdown guide and contains no implementation files.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Operational Monitoring Guidance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts correspond to instructions for checking Make languages, timezones, operation volume, rate limits, and account access. This is expected for Make workflow monitoring and does not collect host system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited guidance queries Make service data, not local OS, network, or hardware details. The context supports a false-positive classification for host reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Service Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires adding an external MCP endpoint for Rube. Users should treat the provider as having access to requests and responses sent through that connection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is explicitly documented. The risk depends on user trust in the external MCP provider, not on hidden behavior in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Make Connection
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup flow requires RUBE_MANAGE_CONNECTIONS with the Make toolkit and instructs users to complete Make authentication when needed. This can grant access to account-scoped operations data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The authentication sequence is explicit and necessary for the skill. It is a real permission risk even though it matches the intended workflow.

content.user_title: Automate Make Operations Through Rube MCP
content.value_statement: Make workflow troubleshooting often requires accurate operation data and valid configuration values. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Rube MCP calls for Make operations, language enums, and timezone enums.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for the Rube MCP endpoint and Make toolkit connection.
Retrieves Make operation records through the available Make operations tool.
Lists supported Make language codes for configuration validation.
Lists supported Make timezone identifiers for schedule configuration.
Suggests filtered operation queries to reduce large result sets.
Describes alternatives when full Make scenario management is required.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP to be configured in the user client.
Requires an active authenticated Make connection before tool calls work.
Does not create, edit, or run Make scenarios through the listed toolkit.
Depends on current Rube tool schemas, which may change over time.

use_cases.title: Monitor Scenario Health
use_cases.description: Review recent Make operation records to find failed runs, high error rates, and unusual usage volume.

use_cases.title: Validate Schedule Settings
use_cases.description: Look up valid timezone identifiers before configuring scheduled workflow behavior in Make.

use_cases.title: Prepare Multilingual Workflows
use_cases.description: Check supported language codes before using language-specific values in Make automation setup.

prompt_templates.title: Check Connection Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the make-automation skill to verify that Rube MCP is available and that my Make toolkit connection is active.

prompt_templates.title: List Valid Timezones
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the make-automation skill to list valid Make timezone identifiers for a workflow that runs in New York and London.

prompt_templates.title: Review Recent Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the make-automation skill to retrieve recent Make operations for the last seven days and summarize failures by scenario.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Tool Replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the make-automation skill to inspect Make operation patterns, then suggest equivalent Rube toolkit steps for the apps involved.

output_examples.input: Find valid timezone values for a scenario that should run in Berlin and Tokyo.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the supported timezone identifiers for Berlin and Tokyo, then advises using the exact returned strings in Make schedule settings.

output_examples.input: Check whether my scenarios had failures this week.
output_examples.output: The response summarizes recent operation status, highlights scenarios with failed operations, and recommends narrower date filters for deeper review.

output_examples.input: Can this skill create a new Make scenario for me?
output_examples.output: The response explains that the listed Make toolkit is limited, then suggests Make native APIs or composed Rube toolkit workflows as alternatives.

best_practices: Search Rube tool schemas before each workflow so parameters match current tool definitions.
Filter operations by date range, scenario, or status to avoid excessive result volume.
Confirm the Make connection status and permission scope before requesting account data.
anti_patterns: Do not assume the Make toolkit can create, edit, or run scenarios without checking current schemas.
Do not fetch broad operation history when a narrow date range will answer the question.
Do not reuse timezone or language values without validating them against the enum tools.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Make account?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires an active Make connection through Rube MCP before Make operation data can be retrieved.

faq.question: Can it create or edit Make scenarios?
faq.answer: No. The documented toolkit focuses on operations, language enums, and timezone enums.

faq.question: Why should tools be searched first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change. Searching first helps Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use current parameters.

faq.question: What data can operation lookups expose?
faq.answer: They may expose scenario execution metadata, status information, usage volume, and error details from the connected Make account.

faq.question: Is the external MCP endpoint required?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written for Rube MCP and instructs users to configure the Rube MCP server endpoint.

faq.question: How should large result sets be handled?
faq.answer: Use date ranges, scenario filters, and status filters when available in the current schema.
